text: Welcome to This is My Architecture.
My name is Christian and today I'm joined by Jean-Paul from Nagra.
Welcome Jean-Paul.
Hello Christian, thanks for having me.
Tell us a little bit about Nagra.
So Nagra provides security and multi-screen user experience solutions
for the monetization of digital media.
I'm in charge of the NexCard B2C team
that works on watermarking, forensic watermarking products,
including for on-demand and live that we discussed today.
Very good. So maybe you want to quickly explain what watermarking is.
Yes, so watermarking is the process of adding an imperceptible mark
to audio or video content, and its goal is to make it traceable,
such that when it leaks, it can be traced back to where it came from.
A typical example is around piracy as seen for the movie or sports industry.
With other industries, they face similar challenges.
I'm curious to learn more about this architecture.
Maybe you can start walking us through and what we have here.
Yes, so the architecture consists of different blocks.
So the first block is the watermark preprocessing.
In this part, we create two variants of the content, A and B.
And these variants, they are visually identical, but they contain a distinct watermark.
The second part is the watermark embedding.
So at that part, we leverage the fact that content, when distributed over the internet,
is typically chunked in small pieces of like two seconds.
And for each piece, we select either the A or the B variant.
And over time, this builds a sequence that is possible to uniquely identify the session.
These two elements, we typically deploy that in a customer environment.
And we have some CloudFormation to make that very easy.
There's a third component, that is the detection service.
This is really a service offered by Nagra.
And this allows you to provide content to the service and extract the A, B sequence inserted.
Can you please explain how you identify these different players with these A, B versions?
So let me give you an example. Let's say we have two players, for example, a mobile device, your PC or connected TV.
They will first connect to a CMS, content management system.
And this content management system will actually grant access.
And the player will select the content to watch.
At this point, the content management system will also select the A, B sequence for each player that will be used for that session.
So for example, the first player gets A, B, A, B. The second player gets A, B, B, A.
This information is provided as a token and will go into the requests to CloudFront.
So for each request, each segment, CloudFront will use the Lambda at Edge to insert a sequence that is encoded in that token.
Okay, so by using CloudFront and Lambda at Edge, you select which sequences of these A, B versions are being delivered to the different players to identify them.
So why did you choose for this package of CloudFront and Lambda at Edge? And what are the benefits of that?
So we were looking for a scalable solution to apply this A, B sequence during the streaming session.
And Lambda at Edge was a good fit and it also allowed us to leverage the CDN caching.
Other solutions like a container or regular Lambda, they were basically not fast enough because they were not close enough to the Edge.
Another thing is that the Lambda at Edge, we have basically a very efficient execution so that we add also minimal latency, which is also great for live content.
And for that one, we're also looking now into CloudFront functions that will scale even better than Lambda at Edge.
I can imagine that the latency is a very critical factor also with regards to user experience here.
We are having DynamoDB in this space over here.
So maybe you can tell us a little bit what you're using it for.
So DynamoDB is actually for a very specific feature that we call a blacklisting.
And the idea is that when the detection service finds basically a certain sequence, for example, A, B, A, B,
it can decide to populate the DynamoDB with the session information.
And at that point, the lab.edge, when it is querying the database, when it is processing the requests,
it can actually decide to block a certain session.
So as a result, the playout for that player, in this case player one, would stop.
So we can terminate the session.
So you have a mechanism to identify if a breach has happened, but you can also shut down the session once it has been identified.
Exactly.
Perfect.
So we've been learning about how you're distributing the content out to the different players and how to the different end users.
So how do you prepare that content actually with these A and B versions?
So the content preparation depends a little bit on the type of content, if it's VOD on demand or if it's live.
But in principle, it's very similar.
And from CloudFront point of view, it's identical.
If we look at VOD, the input is actually a file that will go into an S3 bucket.
This file will then go into MediaConvert.
And MediaConvert is actually the part where the A, B watermark or the variants are created.
So the outputs of MediaConvert will be those A and B versions.
And this goes into the S3 bucket that will then be the origin to CloudFront.
Of course, there are different options.
You could use some scripting or like FFmpeg.
But the benefit of this solution is that it's based on many services that are easier to deploy and use.
And also because you mentioned you're deploying this via CloudFormation automated into the customer accounts,
that's maybe also an easy approach to use those managed services instead of having a complex setup.
Exactly.
So that was for the video on demand content. How does it work for live?
So for live, it's very similar, but the input will be a live feed.
So it will go to MediaConnect that will split the feed into two.
And then MediaLive, where the encoding takes place, we have two channels.
One channel will be the A variant and the other channel will be the B variant.
So it's here where we insert the watermark.
Then MediaPackage will prepare it for Internet distribution and will then be the origin for the CloudFront.
Great. So that is very interesting. What have you planned for the future?
How are you evolving this architecture?
So we are currently looking into data analytics to identify early on if a session is used for piracy.
For example, a session that doesn't terminate and is on a live channel continuously, that's suspicious.
Another area is audio watermarking to not only protect the video content, but to offer the same also for the audio.
Very, very interesting. Thank you so much, Jean-Paul, for sharing this architecture with us today.
Thanks again for having me.
And thank you for watching This Is My Architecture.
